narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 44% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your lower decile rankings in Year 1 and especially Year 3 at school, a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 21, an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, being a Black female planning full-time studies. Compared to typical students—who often rank around the middle or higher in school deciles, score about 37 on the LSAT on average, and have GPAs slightly above yours—your academic markers stood out as weaker predictors of bar success. The strongest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking, which was the lowest possible and far below the average, signaling potential struggles with advanced coursework. Next in influence was your LSAT score, significantly lower than most applicants, which heavily weighed against passing the bar. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, also in the lower range compared to peers. Your middle family income and racial background as Black each nudged the odds further toward failure, as they differ from the majority White, higher-earning profiles in our data that tend to correlate with stronger bar outcomes. Your undergraduate GPA, just shy of the average, and full-time status had smaller but still negative pulls, while your gender as female had the least impact. Overall, these elements combined to tip the model against you, unlike typical admits with stronger academic deciles, higher test scores, and more common demographic matches. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your grades to climb those school deciles through better study habits or tutoring, as that's the top factor you can influence. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score would make a big difference too, given its strong role here. Building a stronger undergraduate record if possible, or gaining relevant experience, could also help offset other factors in future applications. Keep pushing—you have time to strengthen these areas and reapply stronger.
